findings.hypothesis_tested: Plus subscription creates a behavioral paradox where users increase ordering frequency despite heightened cost awareness, driven by genuine economic rationality rather than psychological manipulation

economic_drivers.description: Students who use subscriptions demonstrate rational economic behavior: they calculate per-order savings and maintain subscriptions when the math works

performative_negativity.description: Negative sentiment toward subscriptions coexists with continued usage because students feel social pressure to appear frugal, not because they are genuinely dissatisfied

ordering_frequency_increase.description: Subscription holders explicitly acknowledge ordering more, but frame this as a rational response to lower marginal cost rather than loss of control

bundle_economics.description: Students stack subscriptions (Prime + Deliveroo, Uber One transport + food) to maximize cross-service value

bundle_optimizers.description: Students who stack Prime + Deliveroo or use Uber One for both transport and food
bundle_optimizers.behavior: Subscription value is evaluated across entire ecosystem, not just food delivery

high_frequency_savers.description: Students who order 3+ times/week and achieve genuine per-order savings at scale
high_frequency_savers.behavior: Subscription pays for itself through volume; ordering increase is rational response to lower marginal cost

reluctant_subscribers.description: Students who subscribe for free/trial offers but feel conflicted about continued use
reluctant_subscribers.behavior: Subscribe when free but resist paying; would cancel if cost were introduced

cancelled_subscribers.description: Students who actively cancelled subscriptions due to overuse guilt or budget pressure
cancelled_subscribers.behavior: Cancellation driven by recognizing that subscription encouraged overspending, not by dissatisfaction with service

findings.new_insight: Subscription 'paradox' is actually economic rationality with performative negativity. Three sub-segments exist: bundle optimizers (stacking Prime/Uber transport), high-frequency savers (per-order savings at scale), and reluctant subscribers (subscribe when free but resist paying). Negative sentiment reflects social desirability bias (students feel they 'should' cook more) rather than genuine dissatisfaction with subscription value.
findings.recommendation_update: 1) Show students their actual per-order savings in real-time to reinforce economic rationality. 2) For free-tier users, demonstrate concrete savings they would get from paid upgrade. 3) For cancelled users, offer 'pause' instead of cancel to retain them during guilt periods. 4) For bundle optimizers, strengthen the Prime partnership and explore transport integrations.
